name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ إِنَّ قَـٰرُونَ كَانَ مِن قَوْمِ مُوسَىٰ فَبَغَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْكُنُوزِ مَآ إِنَّ مَفَاتِحَهُۥ لَتَنُوٓأُ بِٱلْعُصْبَةِ أُو۟لِى ٱلْقُوَّةِ إِذْ قَالَ لَهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ لَا تَفْرَحْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْفَرِحِينَ (٧٦)
```

```english
Korah indeed belonged to the people of Moses, but he bullied them. We had given him so much treasures that their chests indeed proved heavy for a band of stalwarts. When his people said to him, ‘Do not boast! Indeed Allah does not like the boasters. (76)
```

```arabic
وَٱبْتَغِ فِيمَآ ءَاتَىٰكَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلدَّارَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۖ وَلَا تَنسَ نَصِيبَكَ مِنَ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَأَحْسِن كَمَآ أَحْسَنَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَلَا تَبْغِ ٱلْفَسَادَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٧٧)
```

```english
Seek the abode of the Hereafter by means of what Allah has given you, while not forgetting your share of this world. Be good [to others] just as Allah has been good to you, and do not try to cause corruption in the land. Indeed Allah does not like the agents of corruption.’ (77)
```

```arabic
قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أُوتِيتُهُۥ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ عِندِىٓ ۚ أَوَلَمْ يَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ أَهْلَكَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ مَنْ هُوَ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُ قُوَّةً وَأَكْثَرُ جَمْعًا ۚ وَلَا يُسْـَٔلُ عَن ذُنُوبِهِمُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٧٨)
```

```english
He said, ‘I have indeed been given [all] this because of the knowledge that I have.’ Did he not know that Allah had already destroyed before him some of the generations who were more powerful than him and greater in amassing [wealth]? The guilty will not be questioned about their sins. (78)
```

```arabic
فَخَرَجَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فِى زِينَتِهِۦ ۖ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُرِيدُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا يَـٰلَيْتَ لَنَا مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ قَـٰرُونُ إِنَّهُۥ لَذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ (٧٩)
```

```english
So he emerged before his people in his finery. Those who desired the life of the world said, ‘We wish we had like what Korah has been given! Indeed he is greatly fortunate.’ (79)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ وَيْلَكُمْ ثَوَابُ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لِّمَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا وَلَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلصَّـٰبِرُونَ (٨٠)
```

```english
Those who were given knowledge said [to them], ‘Woe to you! Allah’s reward is better for someone who has faith and acts righteously, and no one will receive it except the patient.’ (80)
```

```arabic
فَخَسَفْنَا بِهِۦ وَبِدَارِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ فَمَا كَانَ لَهُۥ مِن فِئَةٍ يَنصُرُونَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنتَصِرِينَ (٨١)
```

```english
So We caused the earth to swallow him and his house, and he had no party that might protect him from Allah, nor could he rescue himself. (81)
```

```arabic
وَأَصْبَحَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَمَنَّوْا۟ مَكَانَهُۥ بِٱلْأَمْسِ يَقُولُونَ وَيْكَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَقْدِرُ ۖ لَوْلَآ أَن مَّنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا لَخَسَفَ بِنَا ۖ وَيْكَأَنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨٢)
```

```english
By dawn those who longed to be in his place the day before were saying, ‘Don’t you see that Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes of His servants, and tightens it? Had Allah not shown us favour, He might have made the earth swallow us too. Don’t you see that the faithless do not prosper?’ (82)
```

```arabic
تِلْكَ ٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ نَجْعَلُهَا لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُرِيدُونَ عُلُوًّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فَسَادًا ۚ وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (٨٣)
```

```english
This is the abode of the Hereafter, which We shall grant to those who do not desire to domineer in the earth nor to cause corruption, and the outcome will be in favour of the Godwary. (83)
```

```arabic
مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُۥ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهَا ۖ وَمَن جَآءَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ فَلَا يُجْزَى ٱلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٨٤)
```

```english
Whoever brings virtue shall receive [a reward] better than it, but whoever brings vice—those who commit misdeeds shall not be requited except for what they used to do. (84)
```

## (Commentary)

The story of Qarun from the Children of Israel (بني إسرائيل) was mentioned by Allah, the Glorified, after He recounted the saying of the polytheists: **"If we follow the guidance with you, we will be snatched away from our land"** (28:57). He responded to them with the aforementioned answers so that they might take heed. His state represented their state; then his disbelief in Allah led him to the evil end that it did. Thus, they should beware lest there befall them the like of what befell him. Allah had given him such treasures that their keys would weigh down a company of men possessed of strength. Yet, he imagined that it was he who had gathered it through his own knowledge, the excellence of his thought, and his good management. Consequently, he felt secure from divine punishment, preferred the life of this world over the Hereafter, and sought corruption (الفساد) in the earth. So, Allah caused the earth to swallow him and his dwelling; he had no host to help him against Allah, nor was he of those who could save themselves.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"Indeed Qarun was from the people of Moses, but he tyrannized them. We had given him such treasures that their keys would weigh down a company of men possessed of strength"** (28:76). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: Tyranny (البغي) is seeking haughtiness (العتو) without right. He said: *al-Mafatih* (المفاتح) is the plural of *miftah* (مفتح), and *al-mafatih* (المفاتيح) is the plural of *miftah* (مفتاح); their meaning is the same, referring to that with which locks are opened. He said: *Na’a* (ناء) with his burden means he rose with it despite its heaviness upon him. [End quote]. Others said: The burden *na’a* with him when it weighed him down until it made him lean, and this is more fitting for the verse.

It is also stated in *al-Majma‘*: A company (العصبة) is a group (الجماعة) bound closely together. He said: There is disagreement regarding the meaning of *al-‘usbah*; it was said to be between ten and fifteen, narrated from Mujahid; it was said to be between ten and forty, narrated from Qatadah; it was said to be forty men, narrated from Abu Salih; and it was said to be between three and ten, narrated from Ibn Abbas. It was also said that they are a group who support one another. [End quote]. The saying of the brothers of Joseph: **"while we are a company"** (12:8)—and they were nine individuals—refutes all but the last two opinions.

The meaning is: Qarun was from the Children of Israel, and he sought haughtiness over them without right. We gave him such treasures that their keys would be heavy for a group possessed of strength. A number of exegetes mentioned that "keys" (المفاتح) refers to the storehouses, but that is not quite accurate.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"When his people said to him, ‘Do not exult! Indeed Allah does not like the exultant’"** (28:76). Exultation (الفرح) was interpreted as insolence (البطر), which is a necessary consequence of excessive joy and delight in the trappings of the world. For indeed, it is never free from an intense attachment to the world that causes one to forget the Hereafter and bequeaths insolence and arrogance. Therefore, the Exalted said: **"and not to exult in what He gives you. Allah does not like any swaggering braggart"** (57:23).

For this reason also, He justified the prohibition by saying: **"Indeed Allah does not like the exultant."**

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"Seek the abode of the Hereafter by means of what Allah has given you"** (28:77) to the end of the verse. That is: seek, through the worldly wealth Allah has given you, the cultivation of the abode of the Hereafter by spending it in the way of Allah and placing it in that which contains His pleasure.

His saying: **"and do not forget your share of this world"** (28:77). That is: do not abandon what Allah has apportioned for you and provided you in this world like something forgotten; rather, work with it for your Hereafter, because the reality of a person's share of this world is that which he uses to work for his Hereafter, for that is what remains for him.

It was also said: Its meaning is, do not forget that your share of this world—while it has turned toward you—is but a small portion of what you have been given, such as what you eat, drink, and wear, for example; the rest is a surplus that you will leave for others. So take from it what suffices you and do good with the surplus. This is a good interpretation. There are other interpretations that do not suit the context.

His saying: **"and do good as Allah has done good to you"** (28:77). That is: spend it on others as an act of goodness (إحسانا) just as Allah gave it to you as an act of goodness without you deserving or meriting it. This sentence is of the nature of an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير) to His saying: **"and do not forget your share of this world"** according to the first of the two previous interpretations, and a completion of it according to the second.

His saying: **"and do not seek to cause corruption in the earth. Indeed Allah does not like the agents of corruption"** (28:77). That is: do not seek corruption (الفساد) in the earth by seeking help from the wealth Allah gave you and the status and prestige you acquired through it. Indeed, Allah does not like the agents of corruption (المفسدين) because the foundation of creation is based on righteousness and reform (الصلاح والإصلاح).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"He said, ‘I have indeed been given this because of the knowledge I have’"** (28:78) to the end of the verse. There is no doubt that his saying, **"I have indeed been given this because of the knowledge I have,"** is a response to all that the believers among his people said and the advice they gave him. Their speech was based on the premise that the wealth he possessed was only given to him by Allah as an act of goodness and grace from Him, without merit or desert; thus, it was incumbent upon him to seek the abode of the Hereafter through it, to do good to people with it, and not to cause corruption in the earth through superiority, arrogance, and insolence.

So he responded by denying that it was given to him as an act of goodness without merit, claiming instead that he was given it based on merit due to the knowledge he possessed regarding the methods of acquiring and managing wealth—knowledge which others did not possess. If it was by merit, then he was independent in his ownership and had the right to do with the wealth he acquired as he pleased, using it for various types of enjoyment, expanding his authority and superiority, and reaching his hopes and desires.

This delusion with which Qarun was afflicted and which destroyed him—namely, his claim that what obtained the treasures for him and brought him power and accumulation was his scientific brilliance in acquiring glory and his psychological capability, and nothing else—is a general delusion among the children of this world. Not one of them sees in the glory and borrowed power that destiny has brought him and that apparent causes have facilitated, except that his own self is the agent of it, his knowledge is what led him to it, and his expertise is what maintains it for him.

The Exalted refers to the generality of this delusion and man's natural inclination toward it in His saying: **"When some distress befalls man, he calls Us. Then, when We grant him a blessing from Us, he says, ‘I was given it only because of [my] knowledge.’ Rather it is a test, but most of them do not know. Those who were before them said it [too], but what they used to earn did not avail them. So the evils of what they had earned visited them, and as for those who do wrong from among these, the evils of what they earn will visit them [too], and they will not be able to frustrate [Allah]. Do they not know that Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it? There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith"** (39:49-52). And He said: **"Have they not traveled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? They were more numerous than them and possessed greater power and [left more] traces on the earth, but what they used to earn did not avail them. When their apostles brought them manifest proofs, they exulted in the knowledge they possessed, and they were besieged by what they used to deride"** (40:82-83). Comparing these verses to the story of Qarun leaves no doubt that the meaning of "knowledge" (العلم) in his speech is what we have presented.

In his saying: **"I have indeed been given this"** (إِنَّما أُوتِيتُهُ) without attributing the giving to Allah, the Glorified—as in the saying of those who advised him: **"in what Allah has given you"** (فِيما آتاكَ اللهُ)—there is a kind of turning away from mentioning Him, the Exalted, and a disparagement of the presence of His Majesty.

His saying: **"Did he not know that Allah had destroyed before him some of the generations who were mightier than him in power and greater in amassing [wealth]?"** (28:78). This is a rebuking interrogation and a response to his saying: **"I have indeed been given this because of the knowledge I have,"** using the simplest means by which he might be alerted to the corruption of his statement. For he believed that what he used to acquire wealth, and what preserved it for him and allowed him to enjoy it, was the knowledge he possessed. Yet he knew that among the generations before him were those mightier than him in power and greater in accumulation. Their power and accumulation were also due to "knowledge they possessed" according to his claim, yet Allah destroyed them for their crimes. If the knowledge he was so deluded by and boasted of were the cause that gathered wealth, preserved it, and allowed enjoyment of it—and it were not by the gift of Allah as grace and goodness—it would have saved them from destruction, allowed them to enjoy their wealth, and they would have defended themselves with their power and been victorious through their accumulation.

His saying: **"The guilty will not be questioned about their sins"** (28:78). The apparent context is that it is meant to explain the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) in punishing the guilty and destroying them for their sins. Thus, it is an allusion to not granting them respite or listening to the excuses they fabricate or the humility and repentance they prepare in hopes of salvation. Just as human beings of influence and power, when they wish to punish those they dominate, ask them about their sins to convict them of the crime and then the punishment—and sometimes the criminal diverts their punishment from himself with the excuses he fabricates—Allah, the Glorified, due to His knowledge of the reality of the situation, does not ask the guilty about their sins. Rather, He passes judgment upon them, and a punishment comes to them that cannot be repelled.

Based on this, it appears that the sentence is a completion of the previous rebuke and a response to his attributing his wealth to his knowledge. The gist is that the divine reckoning is not like the reckoning of people, such that if they blame him or advise him, he can divert that from himself with the response he fabricates so as to benefit from his knowledge in that regard. Rather, He, the Glorified, is All-Knowing (عليم) and a Witness (شهيد); He does not ask the criminal about his sin, but rather He takes him to task for his sin; furthermore, He takes him to task suddenly while he is unaware.

This is what the context provides regarding the meaning of the verse. Others have different opinions:

It was said: The meaning of "knowledge" in his saying **"I have indeed been given this because of the knowledge I have"** is the knowledge of the Torah, for he was the most knowledgeable of the Children of Israel regarding it.

It was said: The meaning is the science of alchemy (علم الكيمياء), which he had learned from Moses, Joshua son of Nun, and Caleb son of Jephunneh. The meaning of the knowledge being "with him" is his exclusivity to it over other people, and he had produced a great amount of gold with it.

It was said: The meaning of "knowledge" is the knowledge of extracting treasures and buried hoards, and he had extracted many treasures and hoards with it.

It was said: The meaning of "knowledge" is the knowledge of Allah, the Exalted. The meaning would be: I was given it based on knowledge from Allah and a specific selection by which He intended me. The meaning of his saying **"with me"** (عِنْدِي) is: so it is in my thought and opinion.

It was said: The knowledge is the knowledge of Allah, but in the sense of the "known" (المعلوم). The meaning is: I was given it based on a good that Allah knew was with me. In all these opinions, the preposition "upon" (عَلى) denotes superiority (الاستعلاء), though it was also permitted to be for causation (التعليل).

It was said: The meaning of the "questioning" in His saying **"The guilty will not be questioned about their sins"** is the questioning on the Day of Resurrection. What is negated is the questioning for information (سؤال الاستعلام), because Allah knows their sins better and has no need to ask, and the angels know them from the records of their deeds and recognize them by their marks. As for His saying, the Exalted: **"Stop them! For they must be questioned"** (37:24), it is a questioning of upbraiding and rebuke (سؤال تقريع وتوبيخ), not a questioning for information. It is possible that the questioning in both verses has the same meaning, and the negation and affirmation are due to the different stations (المواقف) on the Day of Resurrection; they are questioned in one station and not in another, so there is no contradiction between the two verses.

It was said: The pronoun in His saying **"about their sins"** refers to those who were "mightier," and the "guilty" refers to others. The meaning would be: the other guilty ones will not be asked about the sins of those whom Allah destroyed from the previous generations.

All of these are interpretive views that do not suit the context.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"So he went out before his people in his finery. Those who desired the life of the world said, ‘Would that we had the like of what Qarun has been given! Indeed he is very fortunate’"** (28:79). Fortune (الحظ) is the portion of happiness and luck.

His saying: **"Those who desired the life of the world"** (28:79). That is: they make it the sought-after goal in their efforts, having no goal beyond it. Thus, they are in ignorance of the Hereafter and the reward Allah has prepared for His servants therein. The Exalted said: **"So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge"** (53:29-30). For this reason, they considered what Qarun was given of wealth to be a great happiness for him without any qualification or condition.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"But those who were given knowledge said, ‘Woe to you! Allah’s reward is better for him who has faith and acts righteously’"** (28:80) etc. Woe (الويل) is destruction; it is used to pray for destruction and as a deterrent against what is not approved. In this context, it is a deterrent against wishing [for the like of Qarun].

Those who said this were the believers, the people of knowledge (أهل العلم) of Allah, addressing those ignorant ones who wished to be given the like of what Qarun was given and considered it an absolute great happiness. Their meaning was that Allah's reward is better for him who has faith and performs righteous deeds (عملاً صالحاً) than what Qarun was given; so if they were righteous believers, let them wish for that.

His saying: **"and none shall be granted it except the patient"** (28:80). Granting (التلقية) is making one understand, and receiving (التلقي) is understanding and taking. The pronoun—according to what they said—refers to the "word" (الكلمة) understood from the context. The meaning is: none understands this word—which is their saying: **"Allah’s reward is better for him who has faith and acts righteously"**—except the patient (الصابرون).

It was said: The pronoun refers to the "conduct" (السيرة) or "path" (الطريقة), and the meaning of being granted it is understanding it or being granted the success (التوفيق) to act upon it.

The patient (الصابرون) are those who clothe themselves with patience (الصبر) during hardships, in performing acts of obedience, and in refraining from acts of disobedience. The reason they are the ones who receive this word, conduct, or path is that the affirmation that the reward of the Hereafter is better than worldly fortune—which is inseparable from faith and righteous deeds that necessitate abandoning many desires and being deprived of many pleasures—cannot be realized except by one who possesses the quality of patience over the bitterness of opposing one's nature and disobeying the soul that commands to evil (النفس الأمارة).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"So We caused the earth to swallow him and his dwelling"** (28:81) to the end of the verse. The two pronouns refer to Qarun, and the sentence is branched from his tyranny.

His saying: **"and he had no host to help him against Allah, nor was he of those who could save themselves"** (28:81). A host (الفئة) is a group that leans toward one another. In "help" (النصر) and "saving oneself" (الانتصار) there is the meaning of prevention and self-prevention. The gist of the meaning is: he had no group to prevent the punishment from him, nor was he of those who could prevent it themselves—contrary to what he used to imagine, that what brings him good and repels evil from him was his power and accumulation which he acquired through his knowledge. His accumulation did not protect him, nor did his power avail him against Allah, and it became clear that Allah, the Glorified, was the one who gave him what He gave him.

The "so" (الفاء) in His saying **"and he had no"** (فَما كانَ) is to branch the sentence from His saying **"So We caused the earth to swallow him"** etc. That is: it became manifest through Our causing the earth to swallow him and his dwelling that his claims of merit and independence from Allah, the Glorified, were false, and that his claim that what brings him good and repels evil is his power and accumulation acquired through his scientific brilliance was also false.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"By morning, those who had sighed for his place the day before were saying, ‘Ah! It is indeed Allah who expands the provision for whomever He wishes of His servants, and tightens it’"** (28:82) etc. They mentioned that *way* (وي) is a word of regret, and it is sometimes used for wonder; both meanings are applicable to the context, though regret comes more readily to mind.

Their saying: **"Ah! It is indeed Allah who expands the provision for whomever He wishes of His servants, and tightens it"** is an admission from them of the falsehood of what Qarun used to claim—and they used to believe him—that power and accumulation in the world are due to a person's brilliance in his knowledge and the excellence of his management, not by grace from Allah, the Glorified; rather, the expansion and tightening of provision are by the will of Allah.

The context is one of realization (التحقيق) rather than comparison (التشبيه) which is suitable for doubt and hesitation. However, they only used "Ah, it is as if" (وَيْكَأَنَ) in their speech to indicate the beginning of their hesitation regarding Qarun's saying, which they had previously accepted and believed; this is a common rhetorical device in usage.

The proof for this is their saying afterward: **"Had Allah not been gracious to us, He would have made it swallow us [too]"** (28:82) in a manner of certainty and realization.

Their saying: **"Ah! It is as if the faithless do not prosper"** (28:82) is a second regret from them and a withdrawal from what was the necessary consequence of their wishing for Qarun's place.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"This is the abode of the Hereafter, which We shall grant to those who do not desire to be highhanded in the earth or to cause corruption, and the outcome will be in favor of the God-conscious"** (28:83). This verse and the one following it are like a conclusion extracted from the story.

His saying: **"This is the abode of the Hereafter"** (تِلْكَ الدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ). The reference to it with the word for the distant (تِلْكَ) is to indicate its nobility, splendor, and the loftiness of its status. This is the evidence that what is meant by it is the happy abode of the Hereafter; thus, they interpreted it as Paradise.

His saying: **"which We shall grant to those who do not desire to be highhanded in the earth or to cause corruption"** (28:83). That is: We reserve it for them. The desire for highhandedness (العلو) is seeking superiority and arrogance over the servants of Allah, and the desire for corruption (الفساد) therein is seeking acts of disobedience to Allah, the Exalted. For Allah built His laws—which are obligations for man—upon the requirements of his primordial nature (الفطرة) and creation. His primordial nature only requires what accords with the best system governing human life on earth. Thus, every act of disobedience leads to corruption in the earth, either directly or indirectly. The Exalted said: **"Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned"** (30:41).

From here it becomes clear that the desire for highhandedness is one of the instances of the desire for corruption; it was singled out and specifically mentioned out of concern for its gravity. The gist of the meaning is: that happy abode of the Hereafter is reserved for those who do not desire corruption in the earth through highhandedness over the servants of Allah, nor through any other act of disobedience.

The verse is general, specified by the saying of the Exalted: **"If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you to a noble entrance"** (4:31).

His saying: **"and the outcome will be in favor of the God-conscious"** (28:83). That is: the praised and beautiful outcome, which is the happy abode of the Hereafter, or the happy outcome in this world and the Hereafter; however, the context of the two verses supports the former.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"Whoever brings a good deed shall receive [a reward] better than it"** (28:84). That is: because it is multiplied for him by grace from Allah. The Exalted said: **"Whoever brings a virtue shall receive ten times its like"** (6:160).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **"and whoever brings an evil deed—those who commit evil deeds shall not be requited except for what they used to do"** (28:84). That is: they will not be given more than what they did, and in this is perfect justice, just as in the requital of a good deed with something better than it is perfect grace.

The apparent requirement in His saying **"those who commit"** (الَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا) etc., would have been a pronoun. Perhaps placing the relative clause in place of the pronoun is an indication that this requital is only for one who frequently commits acts of disobedience and whose sins have encompassed him, as suggested by the plural "evil deeds" (السَّيِّئاتِ) and the phrase **"they used to do"** (كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ) which indicates persistence and continuity. As for one who brings both an evil deed and a good deed, it is hoped that Allah will forgive him, as He said: **"And [there are] others who have confessed to their sins: they have mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil. Maybe Allah will accept their repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful"** (9:102).

Let it be known that the criterion for a good deed (الحسنة) and an evil deed (السيئة) is based on the effect produced by them in the person; by this, actions are called good or evil, and upon this—not upon the external substance of the action which is a type of movement—is a person rewarded or punished. The Exalted said: **"Whether you disclose what is in your souls or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it"** (2:284).

By this, the answer becomes clear to the objection raised against the generality of the verse—that monotheism (التوحيد) is a good deed and it is inconceivable for anything to be better or more excellent than it; thus, the verse is either specific to things other than true beliefs or is specified by monotheism.

The answer is that the effect produced by monotheism can be assumed to have something better than it, even if monotheism does not accept that by way of conceptualization.

Furthermore, monotheism, however it is assumed, accepts intensity and weakness, increase and decrease. If it is multiplied at the time of requital as mentioned before, its multiplied form is better than its non-multiplied form.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi Shaybah in *al-Musannaf*, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas: Qarun was from the people of Moses; he said: He was his cousin and he used to seek knowledge until he gathered knowledge—and he continued in that state until he tyrannized Moses and envied him.

Moses (peace be upon him) said to him: "Indeed Allah has commanded me to take the zakat (الزكاة)," but he refused. He said: "Moses wants to consume your wealth; he brought you the prayer (الصلاة) and brought you things which you bore, so will you bear that you give him your wealth?" They said: "We will not bear it, so what do you see?" He said to them: "I see that I should send for a prostitute from the prostitutes of the Children of Israel, and we will send her to him so she may accuse him of having desired her." So they sent for her and said to her: "We will give you whatever you judge, on condition that you testify against Moses that he committed immorality with you." She said: "Yes."

Qarun came to Moses (peace be upon him) and said: "Gather the Children of Israel and inform them of what your Lord has commanded you." He said: "Yes." So he gathered them and they said to him: "What has your Lord commanded you?" He said: "He commanded me that you worship Allah and not associate anything with Him, and that you maintain ties of kinship, and such and such—and He has commanded me regarding the adulterer, if he commits adultery and is married, that he be stoned." They said: "Even if it were you?" He said: "Yes." They said: "Then you have committed adultery." He said: "I?"

So they sent for the woman and she came. They said: "What do you testify against Moses?" Moses (peace be upon him) said to her: "I adjure you by Allah that you speak the truth." She said: "As for your adjuring me, they called me and set for me a reward on condition that I accuse you of myself—and I testify that you are innocent and that you are the Messenger of Allah."

Moses (peace be upon him) fell down prostrating and weeping. Then Allah revealed to him: "What makes you weep? We have given you authority over the earth, so command it and it will obey you." He raised his head and said: "Take them!" So it took them to their heels. They began saying: "O Moses! O Moses!" He said: "Take them!" So it took them to their necks. They began saying: "O Moses! O Moses!" He said: "Take them!" So it hid them. Then Allah revealed: "O Moses, My servants asked you and implored you, but you did not answer them. By My Might, had they called upon Me, I would have answered them."

Ibn Abbas said: That is His saying, the Exalted: **"So We caused the earth to swallow him and his dwelling"**—he was swallowed down to the lowest earth.

**I say**: It is also narrated therein from Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn Abi Hatim from Ibn Nawfal al-Hashimi the same story, but in it, the woman was brought to the assembly of Qarun to testify before the elite of the Children of Israel against Moses (peace be upon him) regarding immorality and to complain of him to Qarun. She came to him and confessed the truth before the elite. News of that reached Moses (peace be upon him), so he complained of him to his Lord, and Allah gave him authority over him.

Al-Qummi narrated in his *Tafsir* regarding the story that Moses (peace be upon him) came to Qarun and informed him of the ruling of zakat, but he mocked him and expelled him from his house. So he complained of him to his Lord, and Allah gave him authority over him, so He caused the earth to swallow him and his dwelling. The narration is *mawquf* (attributed to a companion/successor) and contains objectionable matters; for that reason, we have omitted its full citation, just as the two narrations of Ibn Abbas and Ibn Nawfal are also *mawquf*.

Furthermore, the narration of Ibn Abbas recounts his tyranny against Moses (peace be upon him), whereas what the verses recount is his tyranny against the Children of Israel. It also indicates that the knowledge he possessed was what he acquired through learning, while the apparent meaning of the verse, as mentioned, is that it was knowledge of the ways of acquiring wealth and the like.

The story is presented in the current Torah in another way. In the sixteenth chapter of the Book of Numbers: "Now Korah the son of Izhar, the son of Kohath, the son of Levi, with Dathan and Abiram the sons of Eliab, and On the son of Peleth, sons of Reuben, took men; and they rose up before Moses with some of the children of Israel, two hundred and fifty leaders of the congregation, representatives of the congregation, men of renown. They gathered together against Moses and Aaron, and said to them, ‘You take too much upon yourselves, for all the congregation is holy, every one of them, and the Lord is among them. Why then do you exalt yourselves above the assembly of the Lord?’

So when Moses heard it, he fell on his face; and he spoke to Korah and all his company, saying, ‘Tomorrow morning the Lord will show who is His and who is holy, and will cause him to come near to Him. That one whom He chooses He will cause to come near to Him. Do this: Take censers, Korah and all your company; put fire in them and put incense in them before the Lord tomorrow, and it shall be that the man whom the Lord chooses is the holy one. You take too much upon yourselves, you sons of Levi!’"

Then the story continues, mentioning their attendance the next day, their bringing of the censers with fire and incense, and their gathering at the door of the Tabernacle of Meeting. Then it is said: "the ground split apart under them, and the earth opened its mouth and swallowed them up, with their households and all the men with Korah, with all their goods. So they and all those with them went down alive into the pit; the earth closed over them, and they perished from from among the assembly. Then all Israel who were around them fled at their cry, for they said, ‘Lest the earth swallow us up also!’ And a fire came out from the Lord and consumed the two hundred and fifty men who were offering incense." [End of the relevant portion].

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **"Indeed Qarun was from the people of Moses"**—and he was his maternal aunt's son: from ‘Ata’ from Ibn Abbas, and it is what is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **"that their keys would weigh down"** (28:76) the verse, he said: The company possessed of strength used to carry the keys to his storehouses.

In *al-Ma‘ani*, with its chain of narration from Musa ibn Isma‘il ibn Musa ibn Ja‘far (peace be upon him), from his father, from his grandfather, from his forefathers, from Ali (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **"and do not forget your share of this world"** (28:77), he said: "Do not forget your health, your strength, your free time, your youth, and your vigor—that you seek the Hereafter through them."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **"So he went out before his people in his finery"** (28:79), he said: "In dyed garments that he dragged along the ground."

In *al-Majma‘*: Zadhan narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) that he used to walk in the markets while he was the ruler, guiding the lost and helping the weak. He would pass by the seller and the grocer, opening the Quran for them and reciting: **"This is the abode of the Hereafter, which We shall grant to those who do not desire to be highhanded in the earth or to cause corruption"** (28:83), and he would say: "This verse was revealed concerning the people of justice and humility among the rulers and the people of power among the rest of the people."

Therein also: Sallam al-A‘raj narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) who said: "A man may be pleased with the strap of his sandal, and thus enter into this verse: **‘This is the abode of the Hereafter...’**"

**I say**: It is narrated from Sayyid Ibn Tawus in *Sa‘d al-Su‘ud* that he narrated it from al-Tabarsi thus: "Indeed, a man may be pleased that the strap of his sandal be better than the strap of his companion's sandal, and thus fall under it."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: al-Mahamili and al-Daylami narrated from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding the verse, he said: "It is tyranny in the earth and taking without right."
